source_description: Employee review from a mid-size technology company, posted 2024
text: The expense system is an absolute nightmare. You have to attach a receipt for literally everything, even a $4 coffee. Then there are three different forms depending on what you're claiming, and it takes 6-8 weeks to get reimbursed. I've given up claiming small things because it's not worth the effort. The whole process seems designed to discourage you from actually using your allowance.

indicators.title: Administrative processes are simplified and accessible
indicators.evidence_basis: Reviewer describes mandatory receipts for low-value items, multiple form types, 6-8 week reimbursement cycle, and self-reported avoidance of claiming due to process complexity.
indicators.reasoning: The inferred criteria for NDI-2cdbgj asks for patterns of administrative burden complaints in public reviews. This review describes a process that requires navigating multiple form types (complexity), applies blanket receipt requirements regardless of value (disproportionate), and has a friction level sufficient to deter employees from completing the process. All three are consistent with the criterion. The reviewer's self-reported avoidance ('I've given up') is a strong behavioural signal of excessive cognitive load.

indicators.title: Expense processes minimize documentation burden
indicators.evidence_basis: Receipt required for $4 purchase; no per-diem or simplified pathway mentioned.
indicators.reasoning: NDI-ypwtie specifically addresses documentation burden in expense processes. The reviewer describes per-item receipt requirements with no indication of per-diem options or flat-rate approaches. The absence of any simplified pathway, combined with the description of the process as designed to 'discourage' use, is a clear negative signal for this indicator.

indicators.title: Inferred signal — recurring administrative burden complaints in employee reviews
indicators.evidence_basis: Single review describing administrative burden. Note: NDI-qqcvqh requires a statistically notable pattern, not a single review.
indicators.reasoning: This review is consistent with the type of complaint NDI-qqcvqh tracks, but the indicator requires a pattern across multiple reviews, not a single data point. A single review can contribute to a pattern assessment but does not on its own satisfy the criterion. This example illustrates the distinction between an individual complaint and a statistically meaningful pattern.

non_indicators.description: The reviewer mentions it 'takes 6-8 weeks to get reimbursed'.
non_indicators.reason: Reimbursement timing is an operational metric, not directly addressed by any current NEI indicator. It may be symptomatic of process complexity (captured by NDI-2cdbgj) but is not itself an indicator criterion.

notes: NDI-qqcvqh is included here to illustrate the difference between a single complaint and a statistically notable pattern. In a real assessment, this review would contribute to a corpus analysis across many reviews, not trigger NDI-qqcvqh on its own.
